symbols: water poured from a shell — the baptismal waters of the Jordan, the sacrament of initiation
divine light from above — the heavens opening and the Holy Spirit descending
Christ kneeling with hands on chest — humble submission to the Father's will
reed cross behind John — the cross that John's baptism points toward
animal-skin garment on John — the wilderness prophet in the spirit of Elijah
river setting with misty mountains — the Jordan River, boundary between wilderness and promise
description: A vertical composition showing two figures in the Jordan River. Christ kneels on a rock in the shallow water at center-left, bare-chested in a white loin cloth, his hands crossed over his chest and his face upturned with eyes gazing heavenward. A golden halo surrounds his head. Standing behind and above him, John the Baptist, muscular and wearing an animal-skin garment, pours water from a shell over Christ's head with his raised right hand. A tall reed cross stands in the water at right. Brilliant rays of divine light stream down from an opening in the cloudy sky above.
theological_description: The Baptism of Christ in the Jordan is the theophanic event that inaugurates Jesus' public ministry. All four Gospels record it, and it is the definitive revelation of the Trinity: the Son stands in the water, the Spirit descends as a dove, and the Father's voice declares, 'This is my beloved Son, with whom I am well pleased' (MAT.3.17). Christ's submission to John's baptism of repentance — though He is without sin — is an act of solidarity with sinful humanity, what Paul later describes as God 'made him to be sin who knew no sin, so that in him we might become the righteousness of God' (2CO.5.21). The Fathers understood the Baptism as Christ sanctifying the waters for all future baptisms; by entering the Jordan, He reversed the curse upon creation and opened the way for sacramental regeneration. John's protest that he should be baptized by Jesus (MAT.3.14) underscores the paradox: the sinless one submits to the sinner's rite to 'fulfill all righteousness' (MAT.3.15).

Jesus baptized by John, the Spirit descending like a dove, the Father's declaration
John's testimony: Behold the Lamb of God; I saw the Spirit descend on him
God made him who had no sin to be sin for us — the sinless one submitting to baptism
